meta.title: Relevance of Pathology Informatics: Logic, Workflow, and the Future of Practice

slides.title: Relevance of Pathology Informatics
slides.subtitle: Logic, Workflow, and the Future of Practice

slides.title: The Work Is Clinical — The Optimization Is Informatics
bullets: Case assembly and "what belongs together"
Stain ordering logic (rules + exceptions)
Report distribution (who must know, when)
Amendments (error detection + correction propagation)

takeaway.text: The clinical work is inseparable from information flow. Technology makes optimization possible; informatics makes it correct.
takeaway.comment: You don’t ‘do informatics’ by clicking buttons. You face informatics problems whenever you have to reason about identity, meaning, and downstream consequences before you act. Informatics is how we redesign that information flow so the system carries more of the cognitive burden — enabling safe, measurable optimization.

slides.title: Poll: Where Do Systems Help vs Hurt You?
slides.prompt: Where do your current systems most help — and most hurt — your work? (click to tally responses)
slides.note: Save results — we'll revisit the top pain points later.

left.heading: Informatics
bullets: Designs information structures + workflows to improve outcomes
Optimizes the "human + system" loop
Clinical logic made operational

right.heading: IT
bullets: Operates infrastructure, security, devices, networks
Installs, maintains, ensures uptime
Keeps the plumbing running

takeaway.text: Informatics = clinical logic made operational. IT = infrastructure. Both essential — different jobs.
takeaway.comment: When a reflex stain doesn't fire correctly, that's an informatics problem (logic). When the network is down, that's IT (infrastructure). Conflating the two leads to the wrong people solving the wrong problems.

slides.title: The Car Analogy: Focus on the Right Abstraction Layer
slides.subtitle: Abstraction layers are key to understanding how computers enforce identity, state, constraints, and interfaces.

takeaway.text: Focus on abstractions that enforce workflow logic and decision layers, where clinical value lives.
takeaway.comment: Example: “Specimen” isn’t just a record—it has states (received, processed, pending add-on, ready for sign-out) and decision layers (reflex IHC, molecular trigger rules, mismatch holds). Encode those correctly and you get safer automation and fewer delays.

slides.title: Thesis: Pathology Informatics Is Built on “Logical Sciences”
slides.subtitle: Lincoln & Korpman (Science, 1980): a hybrid of medicine + logical sciences enabled by computers — the semantics matter more than the hardware.
bullets: Logical sciences here ≠ programming mechanics; they are formal + cognitive disciplines that define meaning: representation, inference, decision, and system behavior.
In pathology, the hardest problems are semantic: defining entities (case/part/block/slide), time, provenance, responsibility, and the constraints that make data computable and safe.
Workflow automation succeeds only when states and transitions have explicit clinical meaning ("completed" ≠ "clinically ready"; exceptions are first-class, not afterthoughts).
Decision theory + statistics govern alerting: tuning sensitivity/specificity to the clinical cost of error (laterality discordance ≠ typographic noise).
Behavioral science/human factors are part of the logic: systems must model how clinicians actually work, not how software prefers them to work.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
numberedItems: Describe lab workflow as a system of events and decisions
Explain why automation should reduce cognitive load, not create it
Identify how data representation and quality drive safety, analytics, and AI performance
Understand how LLMs and computer vision AI are reshaping pathology practice
Ask better questions of vendors/IT: "show me how exceptions work"

slides.title: What "Workflow" Means in the Lab
slides.subtitle: The chain of tasks and events that creates clinical value.
bullets: "Workflow is why your day feels smooth or chaotic"
It is the substrate on which everything else depends — data, automation, AI
Good workflow design: invisible. Bad workflow design: constant friction

takeaway.text: Workflow is the substrate on which everything else (data, automation, AI) depends.
takeaway.comment: You cannot layer AI on broken workflow. If case assembly is chaotic, an AI that prioritizes your worklist will just shuffle chaos faster. Fix the substrate first.

slides.title: Three-Phase View: Pre-Analytical → Analytical → Post-Analytical
slides.subtitle: In AP these boundaries blur — grossing carries pre-analytical identity risk inside the analytical phase; digital scanning is analytical but creates post-analytical versioning and availability consequences.

takeaway.text: In AP, phase boundaries are not clean — systems must account for steps that straddle phases.
takeaway.comment: Experts genuinely disagree on where analysis begins. Dr. Vinita Parkash (Yale, CDC ECHO 2020) argues the test starts the moment a specimen reaches a professional's hands at grossing — yet grossing also involves triage, identification integrity, and chain-of-custody, which are classically pre-analytical risks. Contamination during grossing (tissue from one case transferred to another) is functionally an identity error — a pre-analytical failure happening inside the analytical phase. Similarly, whole-slide imaging scanning is operationally analytical, but scan failures force rescans, raise versioning questions (which image is the authoritative diagnostic record?), and delay downstream reporting — all post-analytical consequences. Systems that rigidly assign steps to a single phase miss these overlaps and the risks they carry.

slides.title: Micro-Case: Where Did the Delay Happen?
slides.scenario: A surgical pathology case is flagged as "late." You have timestamps across the system but no clear cause. The clinician is frustrated. Where do you look first?
discussion: Systems often measure the wrong step → wrong intervention
"Visibility" is the first step toward improvement
TAT metrics without phase attribution are nearly useless
The fix might be organizational, not technical

slides.title: AP Workflow Steps Residents Should Recognize
bullets: Intake / accession
Grossing
Histology (processing, embedding, cutting, staining)
Slide logistics & case assembly
Sign-out (primary diagnosis)
Amendments & addenda
Consults & second opinions
Reporting distribution & notifications

takeaway.text: A "case" is an evolving object; systems must track state changes, not just snapshots.
takeaway.comment: A colon biopsy starts as one part, gets additional levels, reflex IHC, maybe molecular — the 'case' at sign-out is fundamentally different from the case at accession. Systems that only store the final state lose the decision history.

slides.title: The "Gap": Why Workarounds Appear
slides.subtitle: Requirements evolve; closed systems don't → gap → workarounds.
bullets: Shadow spreadsheets for tracking
"Please ignore the LIS status"
Manual copy/paste and duplicate data entry
People becoming the integration layer
Sticky notes on monitors as the real workflow engine

takeaway.text: Workarounds are not "user failure" — they're system design evidence.
takeaway.comment: Every sticky note on a monitor, every shared spreadsheet tracking cases 'outside the system,' every 'please ignore this status' message is a requirements document written in frustration. Catalog them — they tell you exactly what the system fails to support.

slides.title: What Workflow Management Systems Should Do
bullets: Track assets and transitions (specimen → blocks → slides → images)
Coordinate humans (handoffs, assignments, escalations)
Identify exceptions (delays, missing steps, mismatches)
Provide meaningful metrics (not vanity timestamps)
Reduce cognitive load and prevent preventable errors

slides.title: Think–Pair–Share: What Would You Automate Tomorrow?
slides.prompt: Write one automation you'd implement tomorrow that would make your work safer or faster. Then discuss with a neighbor.

slides.title: The Problem with "Generic Automation"
slides.subtitle: Automation without domain understanding becomes coercion.
bullets: Forced structured fields that don't match reality → unsafe workarounds
Hard stops that don't understand urgency or context
Status labels that are administratively neat but clinically false
One-size workflows that ignore subspecialty differences

left.heading: Good Automation
bullets: Removes repetition
Surfaces exceptions
Helps triage attention
Makes the right action easier than the wrong one

right.heading: Bad Automation
bullets: Standardizes away nuance
Creates noise and alert fatigue
Forces clinicians to work around the system
Prioritizes administrative neatness over clinical truth

takeaway.text: The goal is not "more automation" — it's better logic.
takeaway.comment: A system that auto-populates a synoptic template from dictation (good) vs one that forces you into a rigid pick-list that can't represent 'involved margin, focal, < 0.1 mm' (bad). The difference is whether the automation was designed with clinical reality in mind.

slides.title: Maturity Ladder: Tracking → Prevention → Guidance
slides.takeaway: The goal is not "more automation," but better logic at each level.

slides.title: Micro-Case: Hard Stop vs Guardrail?
slides.scenario: (1) Laterality mismatch, (2) Wrong-site label risk, (3) Missing clinical correlation, (4) Missing CC recipient. What's the right system response for each?
discussion: No single answer is always right — context matters
Hard stops are expensive (time, attention, trust erosion)
Silent logging misses the intervention window
Guardrails are often the sweet spot — but require good UX
slides.discussionFrame: Framework: Severity × Detectability × Context × Operational Reality

slides.title: Competitive Advantage (Yes, Even in Academia)
bullets: Competitive advantage = measurable superiority in outcomes, efficiency, reliability, experience
Better workflow + better data → fewer delays, fewer amendments, better service to care teams
Labs that master informatics attract talent and clinical partners
This isn't corporate jargon — it's how departments survive and grow

slides.title: Data Exists to Enable Correct Action
slides.subtitle: Data is fuel for decisions, distribution, analytics, and safety.
bullets: Every data element should serve a downstream decision
Bad data forces humans to become the integration engine
"Garbage in, garbage out" applies to AI even more than to reports

takeaway.text: Bad data forces humans to become the integration engine.
takeaway.comment: When laterality is in free text but not a computable field, someone has to manually cross-check the requisition, the gross description, and the final report. That 'someone' is usually a resident or PA — and that's a system failure, not diligence.

left.heading: Narrative (Unstructured)
bullets: Supports nuance and reasoning
Captures uncertainty and context
Natural for pathologist communication
Hard for machines to parse reliably

right.heading: Structured Data
bullets: Supports computation, QA, registries
Enables staging, coding, research reuse
Required for analytics and AI pipelines
Rigid — may not capture exceptions

takeaway.text: A coherent hybrid model matters more than dogma about structure vs narrative.
takeaway.comment: Synoptic reporting captures staging and coding perfectly but struggles with 'the tumor extends to the inked margin in a single focus measuring 0.05 mm — clinical significance uncertain.' The best systems let structured and narrative coexist and cross-reference.

slides.title: Data Representation: Where Logic Breaks
slides.subtitle: "Representation" = assignment of meaning to artifacts.
bullets: Synonyms, negations, hedging ("cannot exclude")
Specimen naming drift ("left pelvic node" vs "pelvic LN, left")
Context missing ("per requisition," "per op note")
Laterality is present in text but not computable
These aren't edge cases — they're daily occurrences

slides.title: Data Quality Dimensions (Why Analytics/AI Fail)
bullets: Completeness — are all required fields populated?
Correctness — does the data match reality?
Timeliness — is it available when needed?
Consistency — same concept, same representation?
slides.note: Clinical consequences: wrong follow-up, incomplete distribution, missed research cohorts, false alarms, alert fatigue.

slides.title: Interactive: Find the Ambiguity
snippets: "Invasive carcinoma cannot be excluded in this limited sample."
"Left? (per requisition)" — laterality uncertain, documented as given.
Narrative says "negative for malignancy." Synoptic field: Tumor Present = Yes.
slides.prompt: What would a system need to know to prevent downstream error in each case?
slides.goal: Make residents articulate context dependencies explicitly.

slides.title: Data Flow Lifecycle: Creation → Storage → Use

takeaway.text: Failures cluster at interfaces, not in isolated steps.
takeaway.comment: The grossing station works fine. The histology lab works fine. But the handoff between them — where cassette labels must match accession numbers, part designators, and block codes — is where mismatches occur. Most errors live in the seams.

slides.title: The Pathology Systems Ecosystem
slides.subtitle: No single system — pathology runs on an interconnected network.

takeaway.text: Pathology runs on a network of systems. Integration quality determines clinical quality.
takeaway.comment: LIS, EHR, PACS, molecular platforms, reference lab portals, billing systems — a single case may touch 5+ systems. If they don't share identity and state reliably, the pathologist becomes the human integration bus.

slides.title: Stewardship: Pathologist Responsibility Expands
slides.subtitle: Laboratory information stewardship = integrity + optimal use of lab data.
bullets: You may not own IT, but you own the clinical consequences
Configuration decisions shape patient care daily
Who validates that reflex rules fire correctly? Who checks distribution logic?
Stewardship is not optional — it's core competency

takeaway.text: You may not own IT, but you own clinical consequences.
takeaway.comment: When a report goes to the wrong provider because distribution rules weren't updated after a service change, IT didn't make the clinical error — but the patient was harmed. Pathologists must own the configuration decisions that affect patient care.

slides.title: Organizational Reality: Governance and "Who Decides"
bullets: Priorities, configuration decisions, safety constraints, change control
Relationship to CIO/CMIO and institutional standards
Understanding decision pathways prevents years of suffering
The informatics-savvy pathologist has outsized influence here

slides.title: Configuration Is Clinical Practice
slides.subtitle: Not technical preferences — these shape patient care.
bullets: Ordering catalogs and synonyms
Reflex testing logic
Status definitions and state transitions
Alert thresholds and notification rules
Distribution rules and recipient logic
Access controls and audit strategy

slides.title: Workshop: 10 Questions for a New LIS/Vendor
slides.instruction: Use as an interactive checklist — ask residents to add 2 more.

groups.heading: Workflow
items: "Show me how exceptions are handled — not the happy path."
"How do you handle evolving cases (new stains, rework, addenda)?"

groups.heading: Safety
items: "How do you prevent wrong-patient / wrong-site propagation?"
"What is your approach to hard stops vs guardrails?"

groups.heading: Data + Analytics
items: "Can we compute key metrics without custom extracts?"
"How do you represent laterality, site, and uncertainty in computable form?"

groups.heading: Interop & Usability
items: "What functions integrate end-to-end, not just export data?"
"How do you measure clicks/time-to-complete and reduce cognitive load?"
"How quickly can we change configuration safely (change control, validation)?"

slides.title: Poll: Do You Use Digital Slides Today?
slides.prompt: What is your current experience with digital pathology?
slides.note: We'll tailor the AI discussion based on where the room is.

slides.title: Digital Pathology: Infrastructure Reality
bullets: WSI scanners — high-resolution whole-slide imaging at 40×
Storage at scale — terabytes per year; on-prem vs cloud tradeoffs
Viewers + LIS integration — the diagnostic interface must be seamless
"Digital-first" vs "digital-available" — different maturity, different challenges
New failure modes: identity management, version control, color calibration, uptime

takeaway.text: Digital pathology is an infrastructure commitment, not just a scanner purchase.
takeaway.comment: A single whole-slide image at 40× is ~2-3 GB. A mid-size lab generates thousands per week. You need petabyte-scale storage, reliable networking, color-calibrated displays, viewer integration with your LIS, and uptime guarantees — before a single diagnosis is rendered digitally.

slides.title: Computer Vision AI in Pathology
bullets: What it does: detection, segmentation, quantification, grading assistance
FDA-authorized examples: Paige Prostate (2021) — first assistive AI for pathology, 7.3% improvement in cancer detection (performance numbers are setting-specific)
Key principle: accuracy alone is insufficient — workflow integration determines clinical value
Validation burden: institutions must validate independently, not just trust vendor claims
AI as adjunctive tool: pathologist must review — on-label use matters

slides.title: LLMs and Language AI in Pathology

left.heading: What LLMs Can Do Today
bullets: Structured data extraction from narrative reports
Report drafting and synoptic population
Literature summarization and synthesis
Clinical decision support via natural language
NLP on path reports for registry/cohort identification
Automated coding suggestions

right.heading: What They Cannot Do Reliably
bullets: Replace pathologist judgment
Guarantee factual accuracy (hallucination risk)
Handle novel or rare entities without training data
Understand institutional context and local policies
Self-validate — they don't know what they don't know

takeaway.text: LLMs are powerful text engines — you must understand their failure modes to use them safely.
takeaway.comment: An LLM can draft a perfectly formatted synoptic from your dictation — and confidently hallucinate a lymph node count. It doesn't 'know' anatomy; it predicts plausible text. Treat LLM output like a first-year resident's draft: always review, never trust blindly.

slides.title: AI Governance: Who Validates, Who Is Liable?
bullets: On-label vs off-label: using AI outside its approved indication changes the risk profile
Validation responsibility: vendor claims ≠ institutional validation — you must verify
Bias: training data composition determines performance across demographics and tissue types
Documentation: when AI contributes to diagnosis, what goes in the record?
Stewardship connection: AI governance is the extension of lab information stewardship

slides.title: The AI Maturity Ladder for Pathology
slides.subtitle: Parallels the automation maturity ladder — same principle, higher stakes.

takeaway.text: Don't skip levels. Retrospective analytics must work before you trust real-time AI.
takeaway.comment: If your lab can't reliably measure turnaround time by phase (because timestamps are inconsistent), you're not ready for an AI that auto-triages cases in real time. Each maturity level validates the data and logic the next level depends on.

slides.title: Interactive: Would You Trust This AI Output?
slides.scenario: (1) AI flags a prostate biopsy region as suspicious. (2) An LLM drafts a synoptic from your dictation. (3) An algorithm auto-prioritizes your worklist. What's your response?
discussion: Context matters: What's the AI's track record? Is it validated here?
Trust calibration: Over-trust is as dangerous as under-trust
Transparency: Can you see WHY the AI made that decision?
Accountability: If the AI is wrong, whose name is on the report?
The answer should almost always involve human review — for now

slides.title: Cybersecurity + Availability: The Silent Curriculum
bullets: Downtime = patient safety event — not just an inconvenience
Security controls shape access and workflows daily
Auditability and traceability matter (especially with AI outputs)
Ransomware and major cyber incidents remain frequent and clinically disruptive; trends fluctuate but the operational risk is persistent
Your downtime procedures are as important as your uptime workflows

takeaway.text: The system must be dependable and accountable.
takeaway.comment: In 2023, a ransomware attack on a major health system forced pathologists to hand-write reports and physically walk them to clinicians. Your downtime procedures — paper requisitions, manual logs, verbal critical values — are not edge cases. They are part of the system.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
items: Informatics is the logic of clinical work expressed in software — focus on the abstraction layer that matters, not the chipset.
Good automation removes repetition and highlights exceptions; it does not force generic workflows on clinical practice.
Data representation and quality determine safety, analytics, and AI usefulness — garbage in, garbage out.
AI tools — whether vision or language — are only as good as their integration, validation, and governance.

cta.label: Call to action:
cta.text: Pick one workflow pain point this month. Describe it as: event → decision → data → consequence. Send me one "automation candidate" and one "ambiguity" you've seen.

slides.title: Questions & Discussion
slides.contact: Peter Gershkovich, MD. MHA — Yale University School of Medicine
